ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn term for saag aloo exists; closest_parent 'curry (food)' FOODON_00002809 used as the nearest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 composite dish code for saag aloo; A0CKP (spinach, cooked) used as the dominant vegetable component anchor. Potato component not separately coded.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for saag aloo or a sufficiently close Indian curry dish composite.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of saag aloo is approximately 200 g, presented as a bowl of spiced spinach and potato curry.

nutrient_source.title: Aloo Palak (Potato and Spinach Curry) — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN reports nutrient values for Aloo Palak (potato and spinach curry), which is the same dish as saag aloo. Values adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition). Per-100g values used directly. Sodium estimated from typical Indian curry preparation as ICMR-NIN reports sodium variably; value cross-checked against USDA SR Legacy spinach and potato components.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for saag aloo as a composite dish. GI estimated at 44 based on the dominant glycemic contributor being boiled potato (GI ~50–56 for waxy/new potato varieties) moderated by the high spinach content (GI ~15), spices, and fat content which slow gastric emptying. Mixed vegetable curry with potato analogues in the University of Sydney GI Database (e.g., potato curry, GI ~44) support this estimate. GL per serving = (44 × 9.0 × 2.0) / 100 = 7.9 g (200 g serving, 9.0 g carbohydrate per 100 g).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from spices and cooking salt
hypertension.guidance: Saag aloo contains approximately 210 mg sodium per 100 g (420 mg per standard 200 g serving); individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation and monitor cumulative sodium intake across the meal, particularly if accompanied by bread or rice.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and moderate carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 44 and 9 g carbohydrate per 100 g, saag aloo produces a low glycemic load (~7.9 per 200 g serving) and is generally suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in standard portions without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice or naan.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, high dietary fibre, and beneficial micronutrients
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Saag aloo is low in saturated fat (0.4 g per 100 g), contains no cholesterol, and provides dietary fibre (2.2 g per 100 g), potassium (320 mg), and iron, making it a cardioprotective vegetable dish suitable for cardiovascular disease management when prepared with minimal added oil and salt.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Saag aloo (also spelled sag aloo or saag alu) is a classic North Indian vegetarian curry made from fresh spinach (saag) and potatoes (aloo), cooked together with aromatic spices including cumin, turmeric, garam masala, garlic, and ginger. The dish is prepared by sautéing onions and spices in oil, adding chopped tomatoes, then simmering cubed potatoes and wilted spinach until tender and well-coated in the spiced sauce. The result is a moist, mildly spiced dish with a deep green colour, earthy flavour, and soft texture. Saag aloo is a staple of Indian home cooking and restaurant menus across South Asia, the United Kingdom, and Singapore, where it is commonly served as a side dish alongside naan, roti, or basmati rice. Nutritionally, it is a low-calorie, low-GI vegetable dish providing dietary fibre, iron, vitamin A, vitamin C, folate, and potassium from the spinach, and complex carbohydrates from the potato. It is naturally vegan, gluten-free, and halal. The low glycemic index (~44) and moderate carbohydrate content make it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is low in saturated fat and cholesterol-free, supporting cardiovascular health. Sodium content is moderate and depends on preparation; restaurant versions may contain more salt than home-cooked versions.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Aloo Palak data, which is the closest approved primary source for this dish. GI is an analogous estimate based on potato curry data from the University of Sydney GI Database. No exact per-100g entry for saag aloo as a restaurant composite dish was found in any approved primary source. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: Selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this dish type. Trans_fat_g is 0.0 as this is a plant-based dish prepared with vegetable oil; confirmed negligible per ICMR-NIN and USDA component data. Cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as this is a vegan dish containing no animal products; confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN. Vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 as this is a vegan dish; naturally zero for plant-based foods with no animal-derived ingredients. Vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 as spinach and potato contain negligible vitamin D; confirmed trace/zero per USDA SR Legacy. GI of 44 is an analogous estimate; direct measurement for saag aloo as a composite dish was not found in the University of Sydney GI Database. Sodium value of 210 mg per 100 g is estimated from typical Indian curry preparation and cross-checked against USDA component data; actual restaurant sodium may vary. The food_name has been standardised to 'Saag Aloo' (the more common English spelling) from the input 'Sag Aloo'; both forms are retained in aliases.
